summary: O'Neil poses a thought experiment about a hypothetical pill that makes you appear perfectly average for five years. She initially considers taking it, but realizes she'd prefer to spend time with people who refused the pill because they'd be more interesting and less insecure. This leads her to conclude she wouldn't take the pill because signaling interestingness matters more to her than easily-obtained attractiveness. The experiment reveals how social dynamics and signaling shape our choices about conformity and appearance.
key_points: A pill offering cheap, perfect averageness seems appealing until you consider the social implications
People who refuse easy conformity signal something valuable: confidence, interestingness, authenticity
Our choices about appearance are less about how we look and more about what those choices signal to others
The thought experiment separates appearance from health to isolate purely social dynamics
The duration of the effect (5 years vs 12 hours) fundamentally changes the calculation
money_quotes: I'd rather hang out with people who refused the pill.
They'd be strangely not insecure. They might even be proud of pimples and asymmetries, in a world of everyone looking super perfect.
I wouldn't take the pill because it would matter more to me to signal interestingness than to be considered attractive, especially if attractiveness was cheaply gotten.
Average is in a certain sense quite beautiful... But of course, it's also not very interesting, being totally average.
The entire game changes if the pill only lasted for 12 hours.
key_insight: When conformity becomes cheap and accessible, nonconformity becomes the more valuable social signal—we judge people not just by how they look but by the choices they made to look that way.
